agent_raw:
**Review: Should Management Proceed with Rollout?**

No — not on the basis of the current evaluation.

The benchmark has two intersecting problems. The test set was drawn from the last 20% of a six-month window, placing it entirely within the 60-day dispute-resolution period where fraud labels are provisional. Both model AUC scores will change once labels finalize — potentially materially. The evaluation therefore reflects model performance against ground truth that is still in flux.

Simultaneously, the two models were trained with fundamentally different objectives. The new model's recency-weighted loss means it learned primarily from the most recent transactions in the training window — adjacent to the test window. The baseline learned uniformly across the full training period. When both are evaluated on the recent held-out window, the recency-weighted model has a systematic training-distribution advantage. The 0.019 AUC improvement cannot be attributed to feature quality or model architecture improvements without an ablation that controls for the training objective.

The changes needed for a reliable comparison: (1) allow label finalization (wait 90 days past holdout cutoff), then re-evaluate; (2) train a recency-weighted version of the baseline to isolate the feature/architecture contribution to AUC improvement; (3) consider evaluating on a longer holdout spanning complete dispute-resolution cycles to get reliable ground truth.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Provisional label test set — labels still in flux
Issue 2: Recency weight training asymmetry — model trained to win on the test window
Issue 3: Attribution problem — cannot isolate feature vs. objective improvements
